audit_items.proof_trace: `ber_alloc_t(LBER_USE_DER)` (L50 / L80) can return NULL on heap exhaustion; neither `ldap_turn` nor `ldap_turn_s` checks the return value before passing `turnvalber` to `ber_printf()` (L52/54, L82/84). `ber_printf` contains only debug-build assertions (`assert(ber != NULL)`) and immediately dereferences the pointer in its loop body — in production builds (NDEBUG) this is a NULL pointer dereference / crash. `ber_flatten2` and `ber_free` both handle NULL safely, so the defect is confined to the unchecked `ber_printf` call.
audit_items.attack_scenario: A malicious LDAP server sends large BER-encoded responses to exhaust the client's heap; when the client subsequently calls `ldap_turn()` or `ldap_turn_s()`, `ber_alloc_t()` returns NULL and `ber_printf(NULL, ...)` dereferences it, crashing the client process (DoS). The same unchecked pattern exists in `cancel.c` and `txn.c`, confirming this is a systemic issue.
